context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 6

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 1st April, 2024
Days Covered: 11 and 12 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Failed Induction [10 points]

(a) [5 pts] In Recitation 11 Problem 3, it may seem like we've proved a stronger theorem:
    False Claim. Every graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges can be 6-colored.
Show that this claim is actually false, by finding some integer n and some graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges that cannot be 6-colored. Be sure to explain why your example cannot be 6-colored.
(b) [5 pts] Why doesn't the induction argument from Recitation 11 Problem 3(c) work for this stronger claim? Where does the proof break down?


Problem 2. High Chromatic Number Without Triangles [15 points]

A basic example of a graph with chromatic number n is the complete graph on n vertices, that is χ(Kn) = n. This implies that any graph with Kn as a subgraph must have chromatic number at least n. It's a common misconception to think that, conversely, graphs with high chromatic number must contain a large complete subgraph. In this problem we exhibit a counterexample: a graph with chromatic number four that contains no triangle (length-three cycle) and hence no subgraph isomorphic to Kn for n ≥ 3. Namely, let G be the 11-vertex graph below. The reader can verify that G is triangle-free.

[Figure: the Grötzsch graph G, an 11-vertex triangle-free graph with χ(G) = 4. It has a central vertex w, an inner 5-cycle v0, v1, v2, v3, v4, and an outer set of 5 vertices u0, u1, u2, u3, u4, with edges connecting w to each vi and each ui to two non-adjacent vj's.]

(a) [6 pts] Prove that χ(G) ≤ 4.
(b) [3 pts] Assume that we had a coloring of G using only 3 colors. Prove that the coloring must match the partial coloring shown below, possibly after rotating the graph and/or relabeling the three colors.
[Figure: partial 3-coloring of the inner wheel — Blue and Pink on the top two outer vertices, Pink and Blue on the lower two, and Green on the central/bottom vertex, with v0..v4 and w labeled.]
(c) [6 pts] Prove that χ(G) > 3.
    Hint: Part (b) might be helpful.


Problem 3. Find A Pet [13 points]

Hasbro's market research has shown that giving their TV show protagonists animal sidekicks can increase toy sales. Meanwhile, 4 ponies were traveling across Equestria on their latest friendship quest and discovered an old animal sanctuary. Four of the local critters decided to join the group.

The following tables show everypony's and everycritter's preferences (most-favored to least-favored):

| Pony                | Critters (preferences) |
|---------------------|------------------------|
| Hitch Trailblazer   | D, C, B, A             |
| Izzy Moonbow        | A, C, B, D             |
| Sunny Starscout     | C, B, D, A             |
| Zipp Storm          | D, B, C, A             |

| Critter                  | Ponies (preferences) |
|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Arlo the Armadillo       | H, S, Z, I           |
| Beatrice the Beaver      | H, I, S, Z           |
| Cassandra the Capybara   | H, I, Z, S           |
| Draco the Dragonfly      | H, Z, S, I           |

(a) [5 pts] Run the Matching Algorithm with critters proposing to ponies. Please specify the proposals (new and recurring) and reservations/rejections that occur on each day. (We recommend drawing a box around the reserved partner on each day.) You may or may not need all the space given.
(b) [5 pts] Run the Matching Algorithm, this time with ponies proposing to pets.
(c) [3 pts] Prove that any matching pairing H with C is unstable.


Problem 4. Stable Merriment [12 points]

In a stable matching between n Pokémon and n Trainers produced by the Stable Matching Algorithm (with Pokémon applying and Trainers evaluating), call a Pokémon or Trainer merry if they are matched with one of their top ⌈n/2⌉ choices. In this problem, we'll prove that at least one Pokémon or Trainer must be merry.
Let S be the total number of times a Trainer rejects a Pokémon.

(a) [6 pts] Prove that if there are no merry Trainers, then S < n^2 / 2.
(b) [6 pts] Conclude that at least one Pokémon or Trainer must be merry.
    Hint: What can be said about S if there are no merry Pokémon?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m working on Problem Set 6 for 6.1200, and I’m most stuck on Problem 1 about the failed induction with graph coloring. I kind of see why the original theorem worked, but I’m not seeing why the stronger claim is false or exactly where the induction breaks.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point! Before diving in, let me ask: what exactly does the stronger claim say? In your own words, what conditions does a graph need to satisfy, and what must be true about it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting fresh on Problem 1. I should use the Socratic method to guide them without giving anything away. The best first step is to make sure they understand what the claim is actually saying, and then probe their understanding of what a counterexample would need to look like. This is a minimal, targeted opening question.

exchanges.student: I think it says: if a graph has \(n\) vertices and at most \(3n-6\) edges, then it should be possible to color the vertices with 6 colors so adjacent vertices get different colors. So it’s claiming that edge count alone is enough to guarantee 6-colorability for every such graph.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. So to disprove it, you need a counterexample — a specific graph that satisfies the edge count condition but *cannot* be 6-colored. What kinds of graphs do you know that require many colors to properly color?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly understood the claim. Now I should push them toward finding a counterexample. The key insight is that they need a graph that satisfies the edge condition but requires more than 6 colors. I'll nudge them to think about what kinds of graphs require many colors, without giving away the answer.

exchanges.student: The first thing I think of is a complete graph, since every vertex touches every other one. I’m a little unsure which \(K_n\) would fit the edge bound and still need more than 6 colors.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct! So, how many edges does K_n have? And separately, how many colors does K_n require to properly color it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track thinking about complete graphs. I should guide them to compute the number of edges in K_n and check when it satisfies the 3n-6 bound, and also when it requires more than 6 colors. These are two separate conditions they need to check simultaneously. I'll ask them to work out the edge count formula first.

exchanges.student: \(K_n\) has \(\frac{n(n-1)}{2}\) edges, and I think it needs \(n\) colors because every pair of vertices is adjacent. So maybe I should look for some \(n \ge 7\) where \(\frac{n(n-1)}{2} \le 3n-6\)?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly the right inequality to check. Go ahead and solve it — which values of n satisfy that condition?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified both facts about K_n and set up the right inequality. I should let them solve the inequality themselves rather than doing it for them.

exchanges.student:
Let me try it:  
\[
\frac{n(n-1)}{2} \le 3n-6
\]
so
\[
n(n-1)\le 6n-12 \Rightarrow n^2-7n+12\le 0 \Rightarrow (n-3)(n-4)\le 0.
\]
So that only works for \(3 \le n \le 4\), which is not enough since I need more than 6 colors. So I guess a complete graph won’t give the counterexample, and now I’m a bit stuck.
exchanges.tutor: Good work — you've correctly ruled out complete graphs. Now think about this: the bound 3n−6 might look familiar from elsewhere in the course. Does it remind you of any theorem or property about graphs you've studied?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly worked out that complete graphs don't work for this counterexample. This is actually a good learning moment. The key insight is that the edge bound 3n-6 is actually the bound for planar graphs — and planar graphs are exactly the graphs that CAN be embedded in a plane without crossings. The student should think about whether there are non-planar graphs that still satisfy the edge count, or think about what the edge bound 3n-6 is actually capturing. I'll nudge them toward thinking about what kind of graphs have at most 3n-6 edges, without giving away the connection to planarity.

exchanges.student: Yeah, it reminds me of planar graphs, since I remember something like planar graphs have at most \(3n-6\) edges for \(n \ge 3\). But I’m not sure how that helps here, because the claim is about all graphs with that many edges, not just planar ones.
exchanges.tutor: You're very close to something important! You said planar graphs have *at most* 3n−6 edges. Does that mean every graph with at most 3n−6 edges is planar? Or could a non-planar graph also satisfy that edge bound?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a crucial connection to planar graphs. Now I need to help them see that the edge bound is a necessary condition for planarity but not sufficient — there exist non-planar graphs that still satisfy the edge count. More importantly, I should guide them to think about whether ALL graphs satisfying 3n-6 are planar, or just planar graphs satisfy 3n-6. This distinction is key to finding a counterexample.

exchanges.student: Oh wait, right — the condition is only one-way. Planar \(\Rightarrow\) at most \(3n-6\) edges, but not the converse. So I should look for a nonplanar graph with relatively few edges that still needs at least 7 colors… I don’t know one off the top of my head.
exchanges.tutor: You don't need to find something exotic. Step back: what's the *minimum* chromatic number that would make the claim false? And do you know any small, concrete graph — even one that IS non-planar — that achieves that chromatic number?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student understands the one-way implication but is now looking for a non-planar graph requiring 7+ colors with at most 3n-6 edges. This is actually harder than necessary. I should redirect them: they don't need 7 colors, they just need MORE than 6. And actually, the simplest counterexample doesn't require finding an exotic graph — they should think about what the chromatic number of K_7 is, but they already ruled that out. Let me nudge them to think more simply: what is the minimum number of colors needed to make the claim fail? And is there a small, well-known graph that needs exactly 7 colors?

exchanges.student: I only need a graph with chromatic number \(7\) or more. So actually \(K_7\) would do for the coloring part, but it has too many edges. I’m blanking on another standard 7-chromatic graph that still has at most \(3n-6\) edges.
exchanges.tutor: You're overcomplicating this a bit. Think about K_7 specifically: it has too many edges, but what if you added more *vertices* (without adding many edges)? How would that affect the edge bound and the chromatic number?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is fixating on finding a 7-chromatic graph, which is a harder path. I should redirect them: the claim says 6-colorable, so any graph needing MORE than 6 colors works — including one needing exactly 7. But more importantly, I should hint that they might be overcomplicating this. The key insight is: can they construct a graph by taking K_7 and removing edges strategically to satisfy the bound while preserving the chromatic number? Or alternatively, nudge them to think about whether there's a simpler construction. Actually, the cleanest counterexample is K_7 itself won't work, but they could think about adding vertices to bring the edge count down. Let me nudge them toward thinking about modifying K_7.

exchanges.student:
Ohh, I think I see it now. If I start with \(K_7\), it already forces 7 colors, and then I can add isolated vertices. That keeps the chromatic number at least 7, but increases \(n\) without increasing the number of edges.

So if the graph has \(K_7\) plus \(k\) isolated vertices, then
\[
n=7+k,\quad e=21.
\]
I need
\[
21 \le 3n-6 = 3(7+k)-6 = 15+3k,
\]
so \(6 \le 3k\), meaning \(k \ge 2\). So with \(K_7\) plus 2 isolated vertices, I get \(n=9\), \(e=21\), and \(3n-6=21\), and it still can’t be 6-colored because the \(K_7\) part needs 7 colors. Is that the right counterexample?
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly right — well done working that out yourself! Now for part (b): think about how the induction argument from Recitation 11 works. What's the key step in that proof, and why would it fail when you try to extend it to this stronger claim?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly found the counterexample through their own reasoning. This is excellent work. I should affirm this and then move them to part (b), which asks where the induction proof breaks down. I should not dwell too long here since the student has clearly understood part (a).

exchanges.student: I think the key step was removing a low-degree vertex, coloring the remaining graph by induction, then adding the vertex back and giving it a color different from its neighbors. I’m not fully seeing the failure, but I guess for the stronger claim we’d need to know there’s always a vertex of degree at most 5, and maybe the edge bound \(e \le 3n-6\) doesn’t force that anymore?
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track. Can you use the edge bound e ≤ 3n−6 to derive an upper bound on the *average* degree? What does that tell you about whether there must be a vertex of degree at most 5?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is very close to the key insight. They correctly identified the structure of the proof and are almost seeing the gap. I should nudge them to think carefully: does the edge bound 3n-6 guarantee a vertex of degree at most 5? They should try to derive a minimum-degree bound from the edge count to see whether it forces a vertex of degree ≤ 5 or ≤ 6.
